summary: Fair audience recording captures a solid late‑80s Omni show with a killer “Franklin’s/Stranger” jam and an inspiring old‑style “Playin’”, though a few numbers feel rushed or thin.
review: The performance is a good snapshot of the Dead in the late ’80s – the band rides strong drum attacks, Brent’s Hammond chops and Jerry’s MIDI‑flavored solos, delivering highlights like the Franklin’s → Stranger transition and a rare, full‑length Playin’. The audience tape picks up the cavernous Omni ambience; it isn’t crystal‑clear but conveys the energy, while some songs (Foolish Heart, Box) come off a bit hurried or lackluster.

ai_rating.rationale: Multiple detailed reviews praise the musical moments and band interplay (70% weight), while recording quality is only fair due to venue acoustics (30% weight). The average user rating is ~3.5, nudged up by strong show highlights.

recording_quality.technical_notes: Venue’s cavernous Omni hall creates some muddiness and limited clarity, but the tape captures strong drum presence and crowd vibe; no major drop‑outs reported.

show_quality.setlist_flow: First set opens mellow with “Let the Good Times Roll” and builds into rock‑heavy numbers; second set leans into deep jams (Franklin’s → Stranger, Playin') and ends with a modest encore. The pacing feels relaxed but occasionally rushed.

band_member_comments.Jerry: Praised for energetic MIDI riffs and strong lead work; occasional vocal flatness and lyric slips noted.
band_member_comments.Phil: Mentioned in crowd chants and praised for solid interplay with the drummers; backing vocals highlighted.
band_member_comments.Bob: Back‑up vocals noted especially on “Gimmie Some Lovin” and “Wharf Rat”.
band_member_comments.Keys: Brent’s Hammond organ shines on “Gimmie Some Lovin”; keyboards add texture to jams.
band_member_comments.Drums: Bill and Mickey receive high marks for intense, powerful playing throughout both sets.
